entries.mnemonic: NEG-ATE: Make it NEGATIVE — cancel out completely

entries.definition: Lack of proper care
entries.example: Criminal negligence charged.
entries.mnemonic: NEGLIGENCE: Carelessness

entries.definition: Failure to take proper care in doing something; carelessness
entries.example: The contractor was held liable for criminal negligence after the bridge collapsed.
entries.mnemonic: NEGLIG-ENCE: NEGLECT + ENCE — the state of neglecting what you should do

entries.definition: A sentimental longing for the past
entries.example: Looking at old photographs filled her with nostalgia.
entries.mnemonic: NOST-ALGIA: Nost (return) + Algos (pain) - painful return to past

entries.definition: A sentimental longing for the past; bittersweet memory of happier times
entries.example: The nostalgia for the Nehruvian era of nation-building often overlooks the economic challenges of that period.
entries.mnemonic: NOST-ALGIA: NOSTOS(return home in Greek) + ALGOS(pain) — the pain of wanting to return

entries.definition: Subtle difference
entries.example: Policy nuances understood.
entries.mnemonic: NUANCE: Shade

entries.definition: A subtle difference in meaning, expression, or sound
entries.example: A good diplomat understands the nuances of language that can make or break a treaty.
entries.mnemonic: NU-ANCE: NEW + ANCE — a new tiny angle that changes everything

entries.definition: Make legally invalid
entries.example: Nullified the order.
entries.mnemonic: NULLIFY: Make nothing

entries.definition: To make legally null and void; to cancel out the effect of something
entries.example: The Supreme Court can nullify any law that violates the basic structure of the Constitution.
entries.mnemonic: NULL-IFY: Make it NULL — reduce to zero effect

entries.definition: Not aware
entries.example: Oblivious to danger.
entries.mnemonic: OBLIVIOUS: Forgetful

entries.definition: Not aware of or concerned about what is happening around one
entries.example: The government appeared oblivious to the suffering of displaced communities.
entries.mnemonic: OBLIVI-OUS: OBLIVION + OUS — full of oblivion, completely unaware

entries.definition: Stubbornly refusing change
entries.example: Obstinate refusal.
entries.mnemonic: OBSTINATE: Resolute

entries.definition: Stubbornly refusing to change one's opinion or action
entries.example: The obstinate refusal of the state to implement court orders drew contempt notices.
entries.mnemonic: OBSTI-NATE: OBSTinate — an OBSTacle that's born NATE, refuses to move

entries.definition: Slow to understand; not sharp or pointed
entries.example: He was too obtuse to understand the subtle hints.
entries.mnemonic: OB-TUSE: Obtuse angle is not sharp

entries.definition: Annoyingly insensitive or slow to understand; not sharp
entries.example: The obtuse refusal to understand farmers' distress made the minister deeply unpopular.
entries.mnemonic: OB-TUSE: OB(blunt) + TUSE — a blunt, thick tool that cannot cut through logic

entries.definition: Supernatural beliefs or practices; hidden from view
entries.example: Ancient civilizations had many occult practices.
entries.mnemonic: OC-CULT: Hidden cult practices

entries.definition: Relating to supernatural beliefs or practices; concealed or hidden
entries.example: Several scamsters exploit occult beliefs to defraud gullible citizens.
entries.mnemonic: OC-CULT: OC(hidden) + CULT — a hidden cult of mysterious practices
entries.usage: Social and legal contexts involving fraud

entries.definition: Extremely unpleasant
entries.example: Odious comparison made.
entries.mnemonic: ODIOUS: Hateful

entries.definition: Extremely unpleasant; repulsive; inspiring hatred
entries.example: The Supreme Court declared caste-based discrimination odious and unconstitutional.
entries.mnemonic: ODI-OUS: OD + I + OUS — ODious like an ODD, deeply offensive smell

entries.definition: Suggesting evil
entries.example: Ominous signs appeared.
entries.mnemonic: OMINOUS: Foreboding

entries.definition: Giving the worrying impression that something bad is going to happen
entries.example: The ominous silence from the border region triggered a high-level security review.
entries.mnemonic: OMINUS: OM(bad omen) + INUS — full of bad omens

entries.definition: Burdensome
entries.example: Onerous duties assigned.
entries.mnemonic: ONEROUS: Burdensome

entries.definition: Involving an amount of effort that is oppressively burdensome
entries.example: The onerous compliance requirements discouraged small businesses from formalising.
entries.mnemonic: ONER-OUS: ONE + OUS — one huge burden that crushes you
entries.usage: Legal, regulatory, and social welfare contexts

entries.definition: Not able to be seen through; not transparent
entries.example: The opaque glass provided privacy in the bathroom.
entries.mnemonic: O-PAQUE: Cannot PEEK through

entries.definition: Not transparent; not clear or easy to understand
entries.example: The opaque funding of political parties undermines electoral accountability.
entries.mnemonic: O-PAQUE: O + PACK — packed so tightly no light passes through

entries.definition: To exclude someone from a group or society
entries.example: The community ostracised him for his controversial views.
entries.mnemonic: OSTRA-CISE: Cast out like an oyster shell

entries.definition: To exclude someone from a society or group
entries.example: Whistleblowers are often ostracised within organisations that fear exposure.
entries.mnemonic: OSTRA-CISE: OYSTER + SLICE — cut out like an oyster from the group

entries.definition: A person who believes war and violence are unjustifiable
entries.example: Gandhi was a famous pacifist who believed in non-violence.
entries.mnemonic: PACI-FIST: Peace (pax) advocate

entries.definition: A person who believes war and violence are unjustifiable
entries.example: Gandhi's pacifist philosophy inspired civil rights movements across the world.
entries.mnemonic: PACIFI-ST: PACIFIC + IST — one who carries the Pacific Ocean of peace

entries.definition: The presence of something in only small quantities
entries.example: There is a paucity of quality teachers in rural schools.
entries.mnemonic: PAU-CITY: Few (paucus) in the city

entries.definition: The presence of something in only small or insufficient quantities
entries.example: The paucity of trained medical professionals in rural areas is a public health crisis.
entries.mnemonic: PAU-CITY: PAU(poor) + CITY — a city poor in resources, severely lacking
entries.usage: Public health, education, and resource management

entries.definition: To spread throughout something; to pervade
entries.example: The smell of coffee permeated the entire house.
entries.mnemonic: PERME-ATE: Pass through (per) the membrane

entries.definition: To spread throughout and be present in every part of
entries.example: Corruption had permeated every level of the procurement system.
entries.mnemonic: PERME-ATE: PERME(pass through) + ATE — ate through every barrier to reach everywhere

entries.definition: Seeming reasonable or probable
entries.example: His explanation sounded plausible.
entries.mnemonic: PLAUS-IBLE: Worthy of applause - believable

entries.definition: Seeming reasonable or probable; superficially convincing
entries.example: The alibi sounded plausible until surveillance footage contradicted it.
entries.mnemonic: PLAUS-IBLE: APPLAUSE + IBLE — getting applause because it sounds convincing

entries.definition: Full and complete in every respect
entries.example: The plenary session was attended by all delegates.
entries.mnemonic: PLEN-ARY: Full (plenus) assembly

entries.definition: Unqualified; absolute; involving all members of a group
entries.example: The plenary session of the UN General Assembly passed the resolution unanimously.
entries.mnemonic: PLEN-ARY: PLENTY + ARY — plenty of everything, fully attended and complete

entries.definition: To divide into two sharply contrasting groups
entries.example: The controversial issue polarised public opinion.
entries.mnemonic: POLAR-ISE: Create polar opposites

entries.definition: To divide into two sharply contrasting groups
entries.example: Inflammatory rhetoric has polarised the electorate along communal lines.
entries.mnemonic: POLAR-ISE: POLAR opposites — push to extreme ends like North and South Poles

entries.definition: To suggest or assume as a basis for reasoning
entries.example: Scientists postulate the existence of dark matter.
entries.mnemonic: POST-ULATE: Put forward a position

entries.definition: To suggest or assume the existence or truth of something as a basis for reasoning
entries.example: The committee postulated that better infrastructure would reduce farmer suicide rates.
entries.mnemonic: POSTUL-ATE: POST + LATE — putting a post up late — assuming something as a starting point

entries.definition: Not securely held; dependent on chance
entries.example: Many workers face precarious employment conditions.
entries.mnemonic: PRE-CARIOUS: Praying (precari) for safety

entries.definition: Not securely held; dangerously likely to fall or collapse; uncertain
entries.example: The precarious livelihoods of gig economy workers lack the protections enjoyed by formal sector employees.
entries.mnemonic: PRE-CARIOUS: Need CARE before it collapses — dangerously unstable

entries.definition: Producing much fruit or many offspring; highly productive
entries.example: The prolific author wrote over fifty novels.
entries.mnemonic: PRO-LIFIC: Pro at life - producing a lot

entries.definition: Present in large numbers or quantities; producing much fruit, foliage, or offspring
entries.example: Premchand was one of the most prolific writers in Hindi literature with over 300 short stories.
entries.mnemonic: PRO-LIFIC: Like LIFE proliferating — producing abundantly

entries.definition: Eager or quick to argue, quarrel, or fight
entries.example: His pugnacious attitude made negotiations difficult.
entries.mnemonic: PUG-NACIOUS: Like a fighting pug dog

entries.definition: Eager or quick to argue, quarrel, or fight
entries.example: The pugnacious leader's confrontational style energised supporters but alienated potential allies.
entries.mnemonic: PUG-NACIOUS: Like a PUG dog ready to fight — always eager for a scrap

entries.definition: A period of isolation to prevent disease spread
entries.example: Travelers were placed in quarantine during the pandemic.
entries.mnemonic: QUARAN-TINE: Forty (quaranta) days isolation

entries.definition: A state of isolation imposed to prevent the spread of disease
entries.example: Strict quarantine measures were enforced at ports to prevent the spread of the virus.
entries.mnemonic: QUAR-ANTINE: QUARANTA(forty days) — the original forty-day isolation period

entries.definition: To suppress or put an end to something
entries.example: The police were called to quell the riot.
entries.mnemonic: QUELL: Quiet and quash the rebellion

entries.definition: To put an end to a rebellion or other disorder by force; to suppress
entries.example: Security forces were deployed to quell the violent protests near the border.
entries.mnemonic: QUELL: QUELL = QUELL the fire — douse and put it out completely
entries.usage: Law enforcement and political order contexts

entries.definition: In a state of inactivity or dormancy
entries.example: The volcano has been quiescent for centuries.
entries.mnemonic: QUIE-SCENT: Quiet and silent

entries.definition: In a state of quietness or inactivity; dormant
entries.example: The volcano had been quiescent for decades before the sudden eruption devastated the region.
entries.mnemonic: QUI-ESCENT: QUIET + ESCENT — growing into quietness and stillness

entries.definition: Representing the most perfect example of something
entries.example: She is the quintessential professional.
entries.mnemonic: QUINT-ESSENTIAL: Fifth essence - purest form

entries.definition: Representing the most perfect or typical example of something
entries.example: Gandhi remains the quintessential symbol of nonviolent resistance.
entries.mnemonic: QUINT-ESSENTIAL: QUINT(five) + ESSENTIAL — the fifth and most essential element

entries.definition: The minimum number required for valid proceedings
entries.example: The meeting cannot proceed without a quorum.
entries.mnemonic: QUOR-UM: Of whom (quorum) we need this many

entries.definition: The minimum number of members required to be present for a meeting to be valid
entries.example: The Lok Sabha could not proceed with business as it lacked a quorum.
entries.mnemonic: QUO-RUM: QUO(how many) + RUM — how many needed before the rum is poured

entries.definition: Bitterness or resentfulness
entries.example: Despite the divorce, they parted without rancour.
entries.mnemonic: RANC-OUR: Rank sour feelings

entries.definition: Bitterness or resentfulness, especially when long-standing
entries.example: Years of political rancour between the two parties made coalition governance impossible.
entries.mnemonic: RAN-COUR: RAN + COUR(core) — bitterness running to the core

entries.definition: To withdraw a former statement or belief formally
entries.example: The witness was forced to recant his testimony.
entries.mnemonic: RE-CANT: Sing (cant) differently again

entries.definition: To withdraw or disavow a previously held statement or belief
entries.example: Under political pressure, the scientist recanted his findings on the health impact of the project.
entries.mnemonic: RE-CANT: RE + CANT(tilt) — tilting back away from what you previously said

entries.definition: To put right; to correct an error
entries.example: The company promised to rectify the mistake.
entries.mnemonic: RECT-IFY: Make right (rectus)

entries.definition: To put right; to correct an error or undesirable situation
entries.example: The government moved swiftly to rectify anomalies in the GST rate structure after industry feedback.
entries.mnemonic: RECT-IFY: Make RECT(right) — correcting what went wrong

entries.definition: To remedy or set right an undesirable situation
entries.example: Citizens seek redress for their grievances.
entries.mnemonic: RE-DRESS: Dress the wound again - fix it

entries.definition: Remedy or compensation for a wrong; to set right
entries.example: Citizens must have accessible mechanisms to seek redress against administrative injustice.
entries.mnemonic: RE-DRESS: RE + DRESS — dress the wound again, correct the wrong

entries.definition: To say something again for emphasis
entries.example: The speaker reiterated the importance of discipline.
entries.mnemonic: RE-ITERATE: Say it again (iterate)

entries.definition: To say or do something again or repeatedly
entries.example: The Supreme Court reiterated its earlier order directing the state to comply immediately.
entries.mnemonic: RE-ITER-ATE: RE + ITERATE — iterate again and again to make the point

entries.definition: To isolate or hide away
entries.example: The jury was sequestered during the trial.
entries.mnemonic: SE-QUESTER: Set aside separately

entries.definition: To isolate or hide away; to take legal possession of assets
entries.example: The court ordered the defendant's assets sequestered pending the outcome of the fraud trial.
entries.mnemonic: SE-QUEST-ER: SE + QUEST + ER — going on a secret quest to hide something away

entries.definition: A supreme ruler; possessing supreme power
entries.example: Every nation has sovereign rights over its territory.
entries.mnemonic: SOVER-EIGN: Super ruler over everyone

entries.definition: A supreme ruler; possessing supreme or ultimate power
entries.example: India is a sovereign, socialist, secular, and democratic republic as declared by the Preamble.
entries.mnemonic: SOVER-EIGN: SOVERE(above) + EIGN — reigning above all others with supreme power

entries.definition: Not flowing or moving; showing no activity
entries.example: The economy remained stagnant for years.
entries.mnemonic: STAGN-ANT: Standing (stagnum) water

entries.definition: Having no current or flow; showing no activity; dull
entries.example: Without investment in infrastructure, rural economies remain stagnant.
entries.mnemonic: STAG-NANT: STAG(male deer) + NANT — the stag stands still, nothing moves

entries.definition: The state of not flowing or developing
entries.example: Economic stagnation led to high unemployment.
entries.mnemonic: STAGN-ATION: State of being stagnant

entries.definition: The state of not flowing or moving; lack of activity or development
entries.example: Economic stagnation led to widespread unemployment and social unrest.
entries.mnemonic: STAGNA-TION: STAGNANT + TION — the condition of being permanently stagnant

entries.definition: Likely to be influenced or harmed by something
entries.example: Young children are susceptible to infections.
entries.mnemonic: SUS-CEPTIBLE: Capable of taking up

entries.definition: Likely to be influenced or harmed by a particular thing
entries.example: Malnourished children are especially susceptible to infectious diseases.
entries.mnemonic: SUSCEP-TIBLE: SUS + CEPT(take) + IBLE — able to be taken by harm without resistance

entries.definition: Perceptible by touch; clear and definite
entries.example: The company showed tangible progress this quarter.
entries.mnemonic: TANG-IBLE: Can be touched (tangere)

entries.definition: Clear and definite enough to be perceived; capable of being touched or felt
entries.example: The Swachh Bharat Mission produced tangible improvements in rural sanitation coverage.
entries.mnemonic: TANG-IBLE: You can TOUCH(tangere in Latin) it — real and concrete

entries.definition: To scold or criticize severely
entries.example: The manager upbraided employees for their carelessness.
entries.mnemonic: UP-BRAID: Braid up harshly - criticize

entries.definition: To find fault with someone severely; to scold harshly
entries.example: The Chief Justice upbraided the state government for its contemptuous disregard of court orders.
entries.mnemonic: UP-BRAID: BRAID someone UP with harsh words — tying them up in criticism

entries.definition: To forcibly pull something from a person's grasp
entries.example: Rebels tried to wrest control from the government.
entries.mnemonic: WREST: Wrist action to grab away

entries.definition: To forcibly pull something from a person's grasp; to obtain with effort
entries.example: The opposition wrested control of three key states in the assembly elections.
entries.mnemonic: WREST: Like WRESTLE — forcefully pulling something away from someone

entries.definition: Characterized by or adapted to very dry conditions
entries.example: Cacti thrive in xeric environments.
entries.mnemonic: XER-IC: Dry (xeros) condition

entries.definition: Relating to or adapted to very dry conditions
entries.example: The xeric vegetation of Rajasthan has adapted remarkably to scarce rainfall.
entries.mnemonic: XERIC: XERO(dry) + IC — iconic dryness, practically zero moisture

entries.definition: A wooden frame joining two animals; a bond
entries.example: The farmers placed the yoke on the oxen.
entries.mnemonic: YOKE: Yoking animals together

entries.definition: A wooden crosspiece fastened over necks of animals; something that oppresses
entries.example: The freedom movement sought to throw off the yoke of colonial domination.
entries.mnemonic: YOKE: A heavy burden placed on your neck — oppressive weight

entries.definition: Fanatical and uncompromising pursuit of ideals
entries.example: Religious zealotry can lead to conflict.
entries.mnemonic: ZEALOT-RY: Behavior of a zealot

entries.definition: Fanaticism; excessive intolerance of opposition
entries.example: Religious zealotry exploited by political actors threatens India's pluralist tradition.
entries.mnemonic: ZEAL-OTRY: ZEAL gone OTR(over the top) + Y — fanatical excess of zeal

entries.definition: A soft gentle breeze
entries.example: A cool zephyr refreshed the summer afternoon.
entries.mnemonic: ZEPHYR: Zeus's gentle breath

entries.definition: A soft gentle breeze; something light and insubstantial
entries.example: The zephyr of economic reform in 1991 grew into the winds of globalisation that transformed India.
entries.mnemonic: ZEPHYR: Like a feather floating — soft and gentle wind

entries.definition: Great enthusiasm and energy
entries.example: She approached life with great zest.
entries.mnemonic: ZEST: Zing and rest combined

entries.definition: Great enthusiasm and energy; the outer peel of a citrus fruit
entries.example: The young IAS officer approached rural development with infectious zest and commitment.
entries.mnemonic: ZEST: Like lemon ZEST adds flavour — adding energy and excitement to life
